articles.title: The Marginal Revolution: Rise and Decline, and the Pending AI Revolution
articles.summary: A free, four-chapter, ~40,000-word work by Tyler Cowen examining the history of marginalism in economics and why it may dwindle as AI reshapes the discipline. Covers Jevons, the slow development of economic science, and what comes next.
takeaways: Marginalism has a rich and underappreciated history
AI is poised to disrupt foundational economic thinking
A substantial read for anyone interested in the economics-AI intersection

articles.title: The 26 Most Important Ideas for 2026
articles.summary: Includes 'The Monks in the Casino,' examining how young people (especially men) are spending unprecedented time alone gambling online, with the share of 20-24 year-olds not in jobs, school, or raising children nearly doubling in the last quarter-century.
takeaways: Young men increasingly isolated and digitally dependent
Online gambling is a new form of social withdrawal
Sobering charts on the trend of disengagement among youth

articles.title: What to Watch in 2026
articles.summary: A collection of essays from Harvard experts on management and society. Highlights include the 'AI Satisfaction Gap' — generative AI boosts quality and efficiency but paradoxically lowers user satisfaction — and other forecasts on business and societal shifts.
takeaways: AI improves output quality but reduces user satisfaction paradoxically
Multiple HBS faculty perspectives on 2026 trends
Useful for business and societal forecasting

articles.title: In Expanding de Sitter Space, Quantum Mechanics Gets Even More Elusive
articles.summary: Our expanding universe's geometry creates deep puzzles for quantum mechanics. Physicists are finding that the shape of de Sitter space makes quantum phenomena harder to pin down than ever.
takeaways: De Sitter geometry complicates quantum mechanics
The expanding universe introduces fundamental measurement problems
Active area of theoretical physics research

articles.title: Are Strings Still Our Best Hope for a Theory of Everything?
articles.summary: A fresh examination of string theory's status in 2026. Surveys the latest developments and whether strings remain the leading candidate for a final theory of physics.
takeaways: String theory debate continues decades after its rise
Alternative unification approaches have gained ground
The question of testability remains central

articles.title: In Math, Rigor Is Vital. But Are Digitized Proofs Taking It Too Far?
articles.summary: As mathematicians formalize proofs in Lean, a tension emerges: does machine-verified rigor enhance mathematics or constrain mathematical creativity? A timely piece on the intersection of AI and pure math.
takeaways: Lean and formal proof assistants are reshaping how math is verified
Machine rigor may come at the cost of intuitive mathematical creativity
Debate ongoing among professional mathematicians

articles.title: The Math That Explains Why Bell Curves Are Everywhere
articles.summary: An accessible deep-dive into the central limit theorem — why normal distributions appear so ubiquitously in nature, society, and science.
takeaways: The central limit theorem underpins the prevalence of normal distributions
Accessible explanation for building quantitative intuition
Connects math to real-world phenomena

articles.title: How 'Tiny Shortcuts' Are Poisoning Science
articles.summary: An investigation into how small methodological compromises — individually harmless-seeming — accumulate to undermine scientific reliability at scale.
takeaways: Small methodological shortcuts compound into systemic unreliability
Institutional incentives drive the problem
A lens for thinking about institutional decay broadly

articles.title: Why Do Humanoid Robots Still Struggle With the Small Stuff?
articles.summary: Despite massive investment, humanoid robots still can't reliably handle everyday tasks. The piece argues the field may need to return to fundamentals rather than scaling up.
takeaways: Scaling hasn't solved dexterous manipulation
Fundamentals-first approach may be needed
Good counterpoint to hype-driven robotics coverage

articles.title: How Writing Changes Mathematical Thought
articles.summary: A Q&A with historian David E. Dunning about mathematical notation as a technology. The argument: the symbols we use don't just record mathematical ideas — they shape and constrain what we can think.
takeaways: Mathematical notation is a cognitive technology, not just a recording tool
History of math notation reveals how thought is shaped by symbols
Applies well beyond mathematics to language and cognition broadly

articles.title: The Jellies That Evolved a Different Way to Keep Time
articles.summary: A pea-sized jellyfish off Japan has an unusual circadian clock that works differently from nearly all other known biological clocks. Raises questions about convergent evolution and the deep history of timekeeping in life.
takeaways: Circadian clocks can evolve independently via different mechanisms
Challenges assumptions about the universality of biological timekeeping
Fascinating case study in convergent evolution

articles.title: Why Haven't We Heard from Extraterrestrials Yet?
articles.summary: A fresh take on the Fermi Paradox, exploring the latest hypotheses for the Great Silence — from the 'dark forest' theory to the possibility that intelligence is far rarer than we assume.
takeaways: Multiple competing explanations for the Fermi Paradox reviewed
Dark forest theory and rare intelligence hypothesis explored
Updated with recent SETI findings

articles.title: Two Is Already Too Many
articles.summary: An investigation into why South Korean fertility rates have plummeted so dramatically. Goes beyond the usual explanations to examine cultural, economic, and institutional factors unique to Korea.
takeaways: South Korea's fertility collapse has deep cultural and institutional roots
Goes beyond standard economic explanations
Relevant to the broader global fertility decline conversation
